emails.subject_line: something we noticed about morris hospital's coding gap
emails.email_body:
Hi Anthony,

Read your post where you simply said "Amen!" - the kind of unfiltered response that comes from a leader who has seen the reality of healthcare operations up close.

Morris Hospital's revenue cycle history, including unposted cash and backlog issues flagged years ago, suggests the coding layer remains a pressure point.

Hospitals your size running at a 6-8% coding gap leave $16-22M in recoverable revenue unclaimed annually.

Open to a 15-minute call this week to walk through what that looks like for Morris specifically?

emails.subject_line: a thought about morris hospital's financial recovery
emails.email_body:
Hey Anthony,

Morris posted a profitable quarter and avoided a county lease default. That is a real operational win. The question is whether the revenue cycle is now positioned to sustain that trajectory.

A rural hospital CMO we worked with in a comparable turnaround situation recovered $3.1M in the first year by automating coding for their top five service lines. Coder headcount stayed flat. CFO visibility improved significantly.

Can I grab 10 minutes to show you what we found about Morris's specific revenue cycle opportunity?

emails.subject_line: last thought on morris hospital's revenue cycle window
emails.email_body:
Hello Anthony,

Morris is in a narrow window. The county committed to keeping the hospital running. A profitable quarter has bought credibility. This is the moment to lock in structural revenue cycle improvements before the next financial cycle begins.

Hospitals that acted in similar windows came out 12-18% stronger on clean claim rates within a year. Those that waited absorbed the same coding gaps as ongoing losses.

Last shot: Tuesday or Thursday work for a quick 15-minute demo of what this looks like for Morris?

Morris is at a pivotal moment. Structural revenue cycle improvement now could define the next three years of financial stability.

Three rural community hospitals I have worked with, all in turnaround or stabilization phases similar to Morris, each approached coding automation differently and each recovered $2-4M annually as a result.

emails.subject_line: chop + coding capacity under payer pressure
emails.email_body:
Hi Kim,

Read your post on the One Big Beautiful Bill Act and safety-net providers. Your analysis of the downstream impact on revenue integrity operations was grounded and specific.

CHOP's payer mix and pediatric complexity create a coding capacity challenge that compounds when policy shifts hit. Specialty-heavy systems like CHOP typically face 10-15% higher coding error rates under payer rule changes than general acute facilities.

Closing that gap has recovered 10-15% net collections for comparable organizations without adding staff.

Curious whether CHOP is prioritizing upstream denial prevention or coding accuracy as the higher-leverage response to the current policy environment?

emails.subject_line: chop's hcc and specialty coding accuracy
emails.email_body:
Hey Kim,

Your year-end Healthcare Finance News contribution flagged the regulatory complexity heading into 2026. You're clearly tracking where the payer pressure points will land.

A revenue integrity AVP at a comparable pediatric academic medical center ran a 90-day pilot on autonomous coding for their highest-acuity service lines. First-pass approval rates went from 74% to 91%. The coding backlog from the prior two quarters cleared in six weeks.

Is managing coding accuracy across CHOP's specialty mix something your team is actively building a solution around?

emails.subject_line: chop's revenue integrity capacity gap
emails.email_body:
Hello Kim,

You're at Becker's this month. The revenue integrity leaders in that room are all navigating the same constraint: protect margin with tighter staff and more payer scrutiny.

The AVPs who build structural capacity in Q2 will have a measurable advantage by Q4. Those managing through manual processes through peak season will absorb compounding denial exposure.

Would it help to see how two pediatric academic medical centers restructured their revenue integrity operations before this year's payer pressure peaked?

Hello Nirmit,

Given your dual clinical-administrative view, you likely see where revenue cycle and care delivery intersect most painfully. Coding accuracy at the physician documentation level is often invisible until it becomes a denial problem.

Systems that automate this layer typically recover 2-3% net revenue without additional headcount.

Would it help to see how similar health systems approached this alongside their clinical leadership?

emails.subject_line: something we noticed at baptist memphis
emails.email_body:
Hi Nirmit,

Your Connecting the Dots episode with Andrew Baughen on clinical-administrative alignment resonated. Physician leaders navigating both sides of that divide have a different perspective on where revenue cycle breaks down.

My team works with health systems where physician engagement in coding accuracy is the missing variable. Baptist Memphis's size means small accuracy gaps compound into seven-figure revenue leakage.

Curious whether Baptist is prioritizing physician documentation quality or coding workflow automation right now?

emails.subject_line: a thought about baptist memphis's coding accuracy
emails.email_body:
Hey Nirmit,

Your Becker's session on clinical-administrative alignment addresses a real gap. Physicians who understand the downstream revenue impact of documentation choices are rare.

We partnered with a similar-sized regional health system where physician documentation gaps were driving 18% of preventable denials. Our AI-powered coding technology closed that gap and reduced rework by 40% within 90 days.

Is documentation-driven denial prevention something your team is actively measuring at Baptist?

emails.subject_line: a thought about baptist memphis's revenue cycle
emails.email_body:
Hello Nirmit,

Given your dual clinical-administrative view, you likely see where revenue cycle and care delivery intersect most painfully. Coding accuracy at the physician documentation level is often invisible until it becomes a denial problem.

Systems that deploy autonomous coding technology typically recover 2-3% net revenue without additional headcount.

Would it help to see how similar health systems approached this alongside their clinical leadership?

emails.subject_line: something we noticed at insight health systems
emails.email_body:
Hi Ali,

Given Insight Health Systems' ambulatory surgery center portfolio, payer mix management is a direct lever on margin. ASCs operating at this scale typically leave 8-12% reimbursement on the table through coding gaps that manual review can't catch at volume.

That gap compounds as payer complexity increases, particularly for outpatient surgical specialties with high CPT specificity requirements.

Curious whether Insight is prioritizing coding accuracy improvements or denial recovery as its primary revenue cycle focus?

emails.subject_line: a thought about insight's coding throughput
emails.email_body:
Hey Ali,

ASC operators your team presented on at Becker's frequently cite coder capacity as the ceiling on volume growth. Add cases, add backlog.

We worked with a multi-site ASC group facing that same constraint. Autonomous coding reduced their per-claim processing time by 60% and improved first-pass acceptance rates by 22 percentage points.

Is coder throughput something that's limiting Insight's ability to scale surgical volume?

emails.subject_line: a thought about insight's revenue cycle
emails.email_body:
Hello Ali,

For ASC operators, the margin story is tight: payer rates are fixed, volume is the lever, and coding accuracy determines whether volume translates to revenue. One without the other leaks.

Systems we support at comparable scale have recovered $1.2-2.8M annually by closing the coding accuracy gap.

Would it help to see how similar ASC organizations have structured this?

emails.subject_line: something we noticed at insight health systems
emails.email_body:
Hi Ali,

Spoke with a COO at a similar multi-site ASC organization who was dealing with a hard ceiling on surgical volume growth. Not capacity, not referrals. Coding throughput was the bottleneck. Every new OR block they added created a backlog their coders couldn't clear within payer timelines.

Based on Insight's ASC portfolio, the same dynamic likely applies.

Curious whether Insight is prioritizing coding throughput or denial reduction as its primary revenue cycle focus right now?

emails.subject_line: a thought about insight's coding throughput
emails.email_body:
Hey Ali,

That same ASC group resolved the throughput issue by deploying autonomous coding across their highest-volume specialties. First-pass acceptance rates improved 22 points and coder overtime dropped 35% within 60 days.

Their COO said the real ROI was volume capacity: they added 15% more cases per quarter without adding headcount.

Is coding throughput something that's limiting Insight's ability to scale surgical volume?

emails.subject_line: a thought about insight's revenue cycle
emails.email_body:
Hello Ali,

The pattern we've seen across ASC operators: volume grows, coding scales manually, margin erodes. The organizations that break that pattern automate at the coding layer, not the billing layer.

Systems we support at comparable scale have recovered $1.2-2.8M annually through this shift.

Would it help to see how similar ASC organizations have structured this?

pairs.company_name: WVU Medicine (AKA West Virginia University Health System)

emails.subject_line: something we noticed about wvu medicine's expansion
emails.email_body:
Hi Carrie,

Read your post about the talent in healthcare and how often it goes unrecognized. That framing stuck with me.

WVU Medicine is absorbing Independence Health System at $800M. Systems at this integration scale consistently see coding volume mismatches during Epic unification. Five new hospitals, one new platform, existing coding team.

Curious whether WVU Medicine is prioritizing coding capacity build-out ahead of the 2026 go-live or planning to address it post-integration?

emails.subject_line: a thought about wvu medicine's epic integration
emails.email_body:
Hey Carrie,

Drilling deeper on that coding-integration gap: the risk isn't the go-live itself, it's the 90-day window after. Claims coded under the old system get reworked; new workflows aren't yet fluent; coders split attention between cleanup and current volume.

Academic health systems running 25+ hospitals see denial rates spike 18-25% in that window, with recovery often taking 6-12 months.

Is WVUM's revenue cycle team actively modeling this risk for the Independence transition?

emails.subject_line: a thought about wvu medicine's 90-day revenue risk
emails.email_body:
Hello Carrie,

At WVU Medicine's revenue scale ($6.7B), a 1% denial rate increase during the integration window translates to $67M in at-risk claims. That is the number system integration leaders should be stress-testing now.

Orgs we've supported through comparable transitions protected 97%+ clean claim rates by pre-positioning coding capacity before go-live, not after.

Would it help to see how similar systems structured their coding operations ahead of multi-hospital Epic migrations?

emails.subject_line: something we noticed about wvu medicine's expansion
emails.email_body:
Hi Carrie,

Read your post about the talent in healthcare and how often it goes unrecognized. That resonated.

Here's a question that keeps coming up in conversations with health system executives mid-integration: what's the one thing that consistently causes $800M infrastructure investments to underperform their revenue projections in year one?

Curious whether WVU Medicine is stress-testing revenue cycle throughput as part of the Independence integration planning?

emails.subject_line: the answer to that wvu medicine question
emails.email_body:
Hey Carrie,

The answer: coding throughput. Every time.

A $7B health system we worked with absorbed three hospitals in 18 months. Epic unified. Claims volume spiked. Coders couldn't keep pace. In the first 90 days post-go-live, denial rates jumped 22% and recovery took 8 months.

The system had budgeted for technology. They hadn't budgeted for coding capacity.

Is throughput during the Independence integration something your team is actively planning for?

emails.subject_line: what this means for wvu medicine
emails.email_body:
Hello Carrie,

For WVU Medicine, the full picture: $6.7B revenue base, five new hospitals, new Epic instance, and a coding team that will be stretched before the ink dries on the integration.

Orgs that pre-position autonomous coding capacity before go-live protect 97%+ clean claim rates through the transition window and come out of integration with stronger throughput than they had going in.

Would it help to see how comparable systems structured this?
